text:
It was a fantastic Friday forTeam GBas they sealed three golds and several other medals to elevate them into fifth in the overall table. Bryony Page became Britain's firstOlympictrampoline champion, while there were also golds in the women's rowing and the equestrian.

British swimmers also continued their fine form in the pool, with Duncan Scott and Ben Proud securing silver. There was also a silver for the men's rowing, but Ollie Wynne-Griffith and Tom George led their race for so long and might have been disappointed not to win gold.

There is hope for further medals for Team GB this weekend, with the track and field events getting underway on Friday afternoon. Stay tuned for all the key updates from Paris.

Pick up your ultimate guide to the Olympics! Going for Gold provides the day-by-day rundown of what's on and when. Clickhereto buy.

Adam Peaty admitted that he has gone through the "worst week of my life" physically after he had to pull out of the 4x100m medley relay final.

The British three-time Olympic gold medallist caughtCovid-19 while in the athletes' village and while he has been back in the pool, struggled throughout his bout of illness.

"It's probably the worst week of my life in terms of how my body is, and that's no exaggeration," he admitted.

"Everything's come at once. Since Covid, it's just every day waking up with a different thing.

"The medical team's been brilliant. I think it's just a victory getting on that start block there. Hopefully, that'll give my body a nice little push for tomorrow."

Julien Alfred is pretty sure that St. Lucia will party long into the night after she secured her country's first-ever Olympic gold medal.

She blitzed the 100-metre race and won at a canter, making history that will be celebrated back home.

"I'm sure they are having a time," Alfred told theBBC. "Playing music, celebrating, drinking beers.

"It feels amazing [to have won gold and a first Olympic medal for St Lucia]. I'm going through the motions right now. It hasn't sunk in as yet.

"I had to go out there, trust myself, trust my coach and most importantly trust God."

Julien Alfred has become the first-ever athlete from St Lucia to win a gold medal, doing so in none other than the women's 100-metre race.

An impressive10.72 seconds was enough to beat out second-placed Sha'Carri Richardson, with Melissa Jefferson third and Team GB's Daryll Neita in fourth.

Alfred won by 0.15s, a considerable distance, to kickstart parties back home and the 180,000 people in St. Lucia now have an Olympic gold medal.

Samuel Reardon, Laviai Nielsen, Alex Haydock-Wilson and Amber Anning from lane eight have taken bronze in the 4x400m mixed relay.

The Americans led throughout but were pipped at the line by the Netherlands.

Team GB set a new national record in the process of their third-placed run, an exceptional effort by all four.

Adam Peaty will not be representing Team GB in the mixed 4x100m medley relay final tonight, despite recovering from Covid-19.

Kathleen Dawson, James Wilby, Anna Hopkin and Duncan Scott will make up the team in Peaty's absence.

There were suggestions that he could return for the 4x100m medley relay, after being spotted back in the pool, but was not named on Team GB's squad list.



Shelly-Ann Fraser-Pryce has been listed as out of the 100m and has not run.

No information has been given as to why the late withdrawal occurred.

She had been complaining earlier in the day about a last-minute rule change that prevented her and Sha'Carri Richardson from entering the warm-up area, as they had not travelled from the Olympic village.

Dina Asher-Smith also won't be in the final, finishing fifth in her semi. American Melissa Jefferson crossed the line first in 10.99 seconds.

Qinwen Zheng made history in her women's singles 6-2 6-3 victory over Donna Vekic, extending China's lead at the top of the medals table.

Zheng became the first Chinese athlete ever to win a singles medal in a tennis event,cruising to victory over her Czech opponent.

She booked her place in the final by stunning top seed and world No. 1Iga Swiatekin the semi-finals, continuing her form to earn a historic gold for the 21-year-old.

Team GB legend Max Whitlock narrowly missed out on a medal in his final Olympic competition.

He was aiming to be the first man to win three consecutive gold medals on the pommel horse, but finished fourth with 15.200 in a very competitive field.

Whitlock, who was pacing up and down while awaiting the score of American Stephen Nedoroscik,which eventually pushed him out of podium contention.

Ireland's Rhys McClenaghan bagged a superb gold to elation inside the Bercy Arena, with a score of 15.533.



Algerian female boxer Imane Khelf has guaranteed herself at least a bronze medalafter a unanimous points victory over Hungary's Luca Anna Hamori.

Khelif, who failed a gender eligibility test last year and has been a subject of contention in Paris, was a comfortable victor and is safe in the knowledge that she will leave the French capital with an Olympic medal.

Hamori touched gloves with her opponent in a mark of respect, with Khelif aiming to put all controversy behind her and go in search of gold for Algeria.

Speaking to world media at the mixed zone after her match, Khelif had just four words to say and declared: "I am a woman!"

Another masterful performance from Simone Biles saw the American claim her third gold of the summer, taking her tally to seven in a career as one of Olympic greats.

The judges awarded the American 15.300, with her second vault given a 14.900.

Biles smiled before she even hit the mat,knowing that another gold was incoming- with the chance of claiming two more on Monday!

Jake Jarman has secured a bronze medal in the men's floor, despite late fears that he was going to be pipped by his fellow Team GB Luke Whitehouse.

Jarman bagged his first gold medal with 14.933, thoughmany felt that Whitehouse was going to perform even betterwith his routine.

However, Whitehouse had to settle for sixth amid joyous Jarman scenes.

Philippines' Carlos Yulo bagged the gold medal, whileIsrael's Artem Dolgopyat won silver.



There's another medal for Team GB!

They've won bronze in the team dressage.

Britain's equestrian team were under plenty of scrutiny ahead of the Games, with the axed Charlotte Dujardin grabbing the headlines in the days before the competition.

But they have managed to secure a medal in Paris.

Germany win gold.

The IBA will pay Angela Carini £39,000 despite her loss to Imane Khelif in the Olympic boxing.

Carini complained that she had never been punched harder in her career than by Khelif, a cisgender woman whose increased levels of testosterone have caused controversy in Paris.

The IBA, which has been critical of Khelif’s involvement, has promised to pay Carini some prize money despite her defeat.

Emma Wilson was guaranteed a medal after reaching the women's windsurfing final.

And the Team GB star secures bronze in a tight fight with her Italian and Israeli opponents.

Italy's Marta Maggetti wins gold.

Becky Moody says she was expecting to have a 10-day holiday in France two weeks ago.

But after Charlotte Dujardin was dropped from the equestrian team following the emergence of a video containing alleged abuse of a horse, Moody was called up at the last moment.

And the Team GB star has recorded a leading round in the dressage team final.

Moody told the BBC: "Two weeks ago I was expecting to come for a lovely 10-day holiday in France, to be inspired, learn, and go home better.

"Having to do this, there's been a bit of nausea but it's been an amazing experience with an incredible team around me."

Team GB star Jeremiah Azu was disqualified from his heat in the men’s 100m sprint at the Olympics.

A false start was called after the 23-year-old was deemed to have gone too early.

Azu claimed he had been prompted to start by a noise from the pole vault event happening over on the other side of the stadium.

He said:

“I’m feeling great, so it’s a shame.

“There’s a lot of crowd noise, the pole vault’s going on, and I reacted to the sound [of that- and they’re saying I can’t run under protest, so I’m going to go back to the team and, hopefully, they can sort it out.

“They said maybe I can run by myself after, but, yeah, I’m just going to wait it out and see.

“It’s an Olympic crowd, they’re so excited. I don’t fault them, but we’re on the start line and somebody’s reacted to something that’s going on [in the pole vault] and it set me off — so I’m going to see what happens now.”



It's another gold for Team GB!

The men's eight team had a little wobble at the very start but powered through to win in over a second.

READ MORE HERE

The women's eight team have won silver in the rowing.

They comfortably beat Australia into fourth but were unable to pip Canada into second.

Dominant Romania won the race by a staggering four seconds.

The dressage team Grand Prix final is underway in Paris.

Team GB will be going for a medal.

France boss Thierry Henry was unhappy with Enzo Millot’s red card in Les Bleus’ Olympics win over Argentina.

A mass brawl broke out after full-time, with Millot red-carded.

“I don’t agree with what happened at the end of the match. My player [Millot] got a red card and I don’t accept it, it shouldn’t happen,” Henry said.

“I couldn’t control what happened. I went to greet the coach and when I turned around, what happened, happened. We won, but I didn’t like what happened at the end with my player.”

READ MORE HERE

Surfing judge Ben Lowe has been sent home from Paris for taking an ‘inappropriate’ photo with competitors.

Lowe was captured posing and smiling with fellow Aussies Ethan Ewing and Bede Durbidge - who were competing in the events he was officiating.

The snap was shared toInstagramwith the caption: "These three Straddie [Stradbroke Island] Boys doing their stuff at the Olympics."

The International Surfing Association has now sent Lowe home.

A statement read: "It is inappropriate for a judge to be interacting in this manner with an athlete and their team."

Imane Khelif’s father insists criticism of his daughter’s presence in the Olympic boxing event has been ‘immoral’.

Khelif’s participation has caused widespread debate. The 25-year-old boxer has higher testosterone levels than the average woman, prompting her to be banned from last year’s World Championship.

Italian opponent Angela Carini withdrew from their first-round fight after only 46 seconds, claiming she had never been punched so hard in her career.

But Amar Khelife has now told critics: “The attacks against her are immoral, it is not fair.

“Imane is a little girl that has loved sport since she was six-years-old, she used to play football.

“These critics and rumours aim to destabilise Imane, they don't want her to be the champion of the world.

“I tell her prove them wrong in the ring and I hope that she will honour Algeria and Arabic countries and win the gold medal.

“She is our role model, and we want to be like her and honour Algeria and Tiaret.”

Khelif has lost nine times during her career. A claim by the IBA president Umar Kremlev that Khelif’s sex test last year showed XY chromosomes - which are mostly only found in men - has not been proven.



Good morning and welcome to LIVE coverage of the Paris Olympics.

We'll have updates on all the best action throughout the day.

So stay tuned!

title: Olympics news: Adam Peaty out of mixed relay final as Imane Khelif issues gender statement | Other | Sport | Express.co.uk
author: Sam Smith, Jack McEachen
description: Express Sport brings you all the latest from the 2024 Paris Olympics.
